shower.thought: We spend the first half of our lives learning who we are, and the second half trying to undo it.
Your laugh is the only sound you make that you've never actually heard.
The moment you decide to change, you've already admitted the person you were wasn't good enough—which is both the most hopeful and saddest realization.
Nobody remembers the time you showed up on time, but everyone remembers the time you were late.
You can't think of a new color because your brain is too busy thinking inside the box of colors you've already seen.
Most of us are just one bad week away from completely changing our entire opinion about ourselves.
The people who seem the most confident are usually the best at faking it, which is technically a confidence skill.
We call it "overthinking" but it's really just anxiety that learned to sound philosophical.
You've forgotten more than you know, and you'll never know which is which.
When you're young, you think you need to figure it all out. When you're old, you realize the people who seemed to have it figured out were just better at pretending.
The saddest part about moving on isn't losing the person—it's realizing how quickly you stopped thinking about them every day.
Every "weird" thing you do in private is probably something millions of other people do too, but you're all keeping it secret from each other.
You're more honest on your deathbed than you'll ever be at a dinner party, which says everything about how we live.
The difference between confidence and delusion is just time and results.
Nostalgia is just grief wearing a prettier dress.
You can't un-know something, so every piece of information you've ever learned is a permanent resident in your skull, whether you wanted it or not.
The people you hate most are usually reflections of the parts of yourself you hate most.
We're all just doing our best impression of someone older, wiser, and more put-together than we actually are.
A bad memory isn't a bad memory—it's just a memory your brain keeps editing to protect your feelings.
The older you get, the faster time moves, which means the closer you are to death, the less time it feels like you have. That math is brutal.

motivational.thought: The only person you're destined to become is the one you decide to be. — Ralph Waldo Emerson
Progress is not about being perfect. It's about being better than yesterday. — Unknown
Your obstacles are not interruptions to your path—they are the path. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Unknown
The man who moves a mountain begins by carrying away small stones. — Confucius
Comfort is a beautiful prison. Most people choose the bars willingly. — Unknown
You don't need permission to become who you're meant to be. — Unknown
Every expert was once a beginner who refused to quit. — Unknown
The quality of your life is determined by the quality of the questions you ask yourself. — Tony Robbins
Waiting for the perfect moment is just procrastination wearing a nicer outfit. — Unknown
Your future self is watching you right now, wondering what you're going to do. — Unknown
Success is the sum of small efforts repeated day in and day out. — Robert Collier
The only way to do great work is to love what you do. — Steve Jobs
Ambition without action is just daydreaming with better PR. — Unknown
You are not behind. You are not late. You are exactly where you need to be to become who you're meant to be. — Unknown
The distance between who you are and who you want to be is just consistency. — Unknown
Fear is just the thing you haven't done yet. — Unknown
What separates the successful from the struggling is not talent—it's the willingness to be uncomfortable. — Unknown
Your potential is infinite, but your time isn't. Choose accordingly. — Unknown
The person you're becoming will thank you for what you're doing right now. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my therapist that nothing makes me happy anymore.
She said that's great progress—last week you didn't care enough to complain about it.

My dentist asked if I floss regularly.
I said, "Only on days I'm planning to see people," and we both knew that wasn't many.

I've started journaling to process my emotions.
Turns out my biggest feeling is regret about starting to journal.

My dad said he was proud of me for the first time.
Turns out he was just relieved I'd finally moved out.

I asked my coworker how his weekend was.
He said he spent it the same way he spends his weekdays—trying to remember why he bothered getting out of bed.

My friend said he's swearing off social media because it's toxic.
I said, "Good idea," and we haven't talked since.

I tried meditation to find inner peace.
I found inner quiet instead, which is just as good and way cheaper than therapy.

My gym membership expired and I didn't renew it.
Not because I gave up on fitness, but because the treadmill was the only thing that understood my need to run away from everything.

I told my mom she'd make a great grandmother.
She said, "I'm not ready for that yet," which hurt more than I expected.

My boss asked why I look so tired.
I said, "Because I've been awake," and he seemed to accept that.

I started taking antidepressants.
They're working great—now I'm sad and tired instead of just sad.

My friend asked if I believe in second chances.
I said yes, which is why I'm still friends with someone who keeps asking me this.

I went to a self-help seminar titled "Change Your Life in 30 Days."
On day 31, my life was exactly the same, but now I was broke too.

My therapist asked about my support system.
I said, "My bed," and she didn't correct me.

I told my friend I've hit a wall in life.
He said, "At least you stopped moving."

My doctor said I need to get more sleep.
I said, "I'm trying," and we both knew I wasn't.

I asked my parents when they knew I was a disappointment.
They said, "We're still not sure," which was somehow worse.

My therapist asked if I have any coping mechanisms.
I said, "Yeah, I avoid my problems," and she said that's not healthy, which proved her point.

I told my friend that life's too short to waste time on negative people.
He said, "So you're leaving?" and I realized the joke was on me.

My insurance company said they'd cover my therapy.
What they didn't mention is they'd charge my copay every time I mention how much I can't afford my copay.
